title: The Stranger's Friend
content:
آ آ آ آ The strangest things and the maddest things, that a man can do or say,
آ آ آ آ To the chaps and fellers and coves Out Back are matters of every day;
آ آ آ آ Maybe on account of the lives they lead, or the life that their hearts discard,
آ آ آ آ But never a fool can be too mad or a ï؟½hard caseï؟½ be too hard.

آ آ آ آ I met him in Bourke in the Union days, with which we have nought to do
آ آ آ آ (Their creed was narrow, their methods crude, but they stuck to ï؟½the causeï؟½ like glue).
آ آ آ آ He came into town from the Lost Soul Run for his grim half-yearly ï؟½bend,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And because of a curious hobby he had, he was known as ï؟½The Strangerï؟½s Friend.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ It is true to the region of adjectives when I say that the spree was ï؟½grim,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ For to go on the spree was a sacred rite, or a heathen rite, to him,
آ آ آ آ To shout for the travellers passing through to the land where the lost soul bakes,
آ آ آ آ Till they all seemed devils of different breeds, and his pockets were filled with snakes.

آ آ آ آ In the joyful mood, in the solemn mood, in his cynical stages too,
آ آ آ آ In the maudlin stage, in the fighting stage, in the stage when all was blue,
آ آ آ آ From the joyful hour when his spree commenced, right through to the awful end,
آ آ آ آ He never lost grip of his ï؟½fixed ideeï؟½ that he was the Strangerï؟½s Friend.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The feller as knows, he can battle around for his bloominï؟½ self,ï؟½ heï؟½d say,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I donï؟½t give a curse for the ï؟½blanksï؟½ I know the hard-up bloke this way;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Send the stranger round, and Iï؟½ll see him through,ï؟½ and, eï؟½en as the bushman spoke,
آ آ آ آ The chaps and fellers would tip the wink to a casual, ï؟½hard-up bloke.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And it wasnï؟½t only a bushmanï؟½s ï؟½bluffï؟½ to the fame of the Friend they scored,
آ آ آ آ For heï؟½d shout the stranger a suit of clothes, and heï؟½d pay for the strangerï؟½s board,
آ آ آ آ The worst of it was that heï؟½d skite all night on the edge of the strangerï؟½s bunk,
آ آ آ آ And never got helplessly drunk himself till heï؟½d got the stranger drunk.

آ آ آ آ And the chaps and the fellers would speculate, by way of a ghastly joke,
آ آ آ آ As to whoï؟½d be caught by the ï؟½jim-jamsï؟½ first, the Friend or the hard-up bloke?
آ آ آ آ And the ï؟½Jokerï؟½ would say that there wasnï؟½t a doubt as to whoï؟½d be damned in the end,
آ آ آ آ When the Devil got hold of a hard-up bloke in the shape of the Strangerï؟½s Friend.

آ آ آ آ It mattered not to the Strangerï؟½s Friend what the rest might say or think,
آ آ آ آ He always held that the hard-up state was due to the curse of drink,
آ آ آ آ To the evils of cards, and of company: ï؟½But a young coveï؟½s built that way,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And I was a bloominï؟½ fool meself when I started out,ï؟½ heï؟½d say.

آ آ آ آ At the end of the spree, in clean white ï؟½moles,ï؟½ clean-shaven, and cool as ice,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d give the stranger a ï؟½bobï؟½ or two, and some straight Out Back advice;
آ آ آ آ Then heï؟½d tramp away for the Lost Soul Run, where the hot dust rose like smoke,
آ آ آ آ Having done his duty to all mankind, for heï؟½d ï؟½stuck to a hard-up bloke.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ll say ï؟½tis a ï؟½song of a sot,ï؟½ perhaps, but the Song of a Sot is true.
آ آ آ آ I have ï؟½battledï؟½ myself, and you know, you chaps, what a man in the bush goes through:
آ آ آ آ Let us hope when the last of his sprees is past, and his cheques and his strength are done,
آ آ آ آ That, amongst the sober and thrifty mates, the Strangerï؟½s Friend has one.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
